findings.description: The HTML has no h1 element. The document title 'Roanoke Rapids Lake Vegetation Survey Report' appears only in a div with class 'page-header', not as an h1. The first heading used is h2 for 'Introduction'. There is also a nested <main> element, which is a structural issue.
findings.evidence:
The PDF title page clearly shows 'Roanoke Rapids Lake Vegetation Survey Report' as the document title, but in HTML it appears as '
Roanoke Rapids Lake Vegetation Survey Report
' rather than an h1.

findings.description: Duplicate/redundant page-header divs appear throughout the HTML mimicking running headers from the PDF, which is acceptable, but the CSS rule 'h1:first-of-type { border-bottom: 3px solid...' never applies since there is no h1.
findings.evidence:
Multiple instances of '
2023 Roanoke Rapids Vegetation Survey Report
' throughout the HTML.

findings.description: The PDF has a typo 'rootlessaquatic' (missing space) which is faithfully preserved in the HTML. This is not an error in conversion but is noted for completeness.
findings.evidence: PDF page 5: 'rootlessaquatic plant species'; HTML: 'rootlessaquatic plant species'

findings.description: The Dominion Energy URL in the PDF is a hyperlink, but in the HTML it is rendered as plain text without an anchor tag.
findings.evidence:
PDF has a clickable link 'https://www.dominionenergy.com/projects-and-facilities/hydroelectric-power-facilities-and-projects/roanoke-rapids-power-station'; HTML renders it as plain text within a

tag.

findings.description: The HTML table adds a 'Scientific Name' column header that is not explicitly labeled in the PDF table. The PDF table has implicit columns but the second column (containing scientific names in italics) has no header text in the source.
findings.evidence: PDF Table 1 has columns: 'Survey Overview', '#', '%' with scientific names appearing as a second unlabeled column. HTML adds '<th>Scientific Name</th>' as an explicit header.

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, creating structural duplication of the wrapper.
findings.evidence: HTML shows '<main>' followed by '<main>' before the content begins.
